findings.issue: No unit tests exist for utils.ts (makeRequest, anySignal, REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS). The makeRequest function is critical auth infrastructure that was modified during the tournament rounds (AbortSignal plumbing, 30s timeout, Bearer auto-attach). It has zero unit-level test coverage. The anySignal helper (signal combination logic) and the timeout behavior are only exercised indirectly via integration tests (AppRouter.test.tsx, InvitationCompletePage.test.tsx). Edge cases such as timeout-triggered abort, external signal abort mid-flight, and the Bearer auto-attachment decision branch are not directly tested.
findings.remediation: Add remote-frontend/src/lib/api/utils.test.ts covering: (1) makeRequest sets Content-Type when not provided; (2) makeRequest auto-attaches Bearer from localStorage when no Authorization header is set; (3) makeRequest does not override explicit Authorization header; (4) anySignal aborts when any input signal aborts; (5) anySignal propagates already-aborted signal immediately; (6) makeRequest timeout aborts fetch after REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS.

findings.issue: initOAuth calls in LoginPage and InvitationPage do not pass an AbortSignal, creating an inconsistency with OAuthCallbackPage and InvitationCompletePage which do pass signals to their API calls. While makeRequest provides its own 30s timeout, the init requests cannot be cancelled if the user navigates away or the component unmounts. The button is disabled during loading (preventing double-submit), and these are full-page views, so unmount is unlikely in practice — but the pattern is inconsistent with the signal plumbing applied elsewhere in this workstream.
findings.remediation: Pass an AbortController.signal to initOAuth in both LoginPage.handleOAuthLogin and InvitationPage.handleOAuthLogin, aborting on component unmount or re-trigger. Alternatively, document that init calls are intentionally un-abortable because they are user-triggered redirects.

findings.issue: InvitationCompletePage success-path redirect (setTimeout + window.location.assign) is not tested. The test at InvitationCompletePage.test.tsx:36-59 verifies that 'Invitation accepted!' is displayed and that sessionStorage/localStorage are cleaned, but does not assert that the 2-second redirect timer fires or that window.location.assign is called with the app base URL. A regression that breaks the redirect (e.g., removing the setTimeout, changing the URL) would not be caught.
findings.remediation: Add a test using vi.useFakeTimers() and a mocked window.location.assign that advances the 2s timer and asserts the redirect URL. Verify cleanup clears the timer on unmount.

findings.issue: oauth.test.ts does not verify that the AbortSignal parameter is forwarded to fetch. The unit tests for oauthApi.init and oauthApi.redeem call them without a signal argument (e.g., line 56: oauthApi.init('github', ..., 'challenge-123') with no 4th arg) and assert only method and body via expect.objectContaining(...). The signal plumbing (added in tournament R13) is only verified at the integration level (AppRouter.test.tsx:134 checks expect.any(AbortSignal)). A regression in oauth.ts that drops the signal from the makeRequest options would not be caught by the unit tests.
findings.remediation: Add unit tests that call oauthApi.init and oauthApi.redeem with an AbortSignal and assert that fetch receives the signal (directly or via anySignal) in its options.

findings.issue: The refresh_token returned by oauthApi.redeem (HandoffRedeemResponse.refresh_token) is never stored or used. Both OAuthCallbackPage and InvitationCompletePage destructure only access_token from the redeem response and store it in localStorage. The refresh_token is discarded. If the access_token expires and client-side refresh is expected, this is a latent bug. If token refresh is handled server-side (e.g., HTTP-only cookies), this is by design. This is pre-existing (not introduced by tournament rounds) but worth flagging for the auth flow's completeness.

PKCE challenge uses hex encoding (bytesToHex) instead of the base64url encoding specified by RFC 7636 S256 (BASE64URL-ENCODE(SHA256(ASCII(code_verifier)))). This is a spec deviation but is intentional and correct for this codebase: the backend's hash_sha256_hex (crates/remote/src/auth/handoff.rs:497) and is_valid_challenge (requires exactly 64 hex chars, handoff.rs:484-486) both expect hex. The client and server are consistent. Flagged only for spec-compliance awareness — a reviewer familiar with RFC 7636 would expect base64url.
